content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. The Quick and the Dead (1995)

**Score:** 44
**Body:** I think it is pretty clear that Sam Raimi is well respected for his visual flair and camera work. The best of his movies feel like macabre cartoons. 'The Quick and the Dead' is vintage Raimi at the height of his powers with a big budget. The most fun thing though is seeing this A list cast subjected to the Bruce Campbell punishment and weird framing. The great Keith David gets an all time great death scene that is trademark Raimi. It is completely unsubtle and unapologetic about pumping the western cliches full of steroids. I had a blast with it.



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Have you seen Darkman?
  - Oh yes. It's great
2. Great movie. 
3. It's a fun movie. The cast is stacked. Hackman is in top form as the villain, Crowe when he was an unknown, and young DiCaprio. Even Gary Sinise is in it. The supporting smaller actors are all familiar faces, too, like Ace Hanlin, and the Shootist. It's good. 
4. All-time classic TNT movie
5. Love this film, I also love 3:10 To Yuma, I read on here once where someone said The Quick And The Dead is like a spiritual sequel where Ben Wade/Cort (Russell Crowe) becomes a preacher to atone for all of the crimes he committed in 3:10 To Yuma, I think that's a cool film theory.
6. This movie I watched in high in school and fell in love with Russel Crowe 😍
7. RedLetterMedia recently did an excellent 3-part Rank Every Sam Raimi Movie discussion, and to his complete surprise, Rich Evans ranked The Quick and the Dead #2 and it was the one he had never seen before. He said he immediately rewatched it, and then rewatched it again.
8. Not a "GREAT FILM" , but definitely a Great Film. And some actors really got their start here, that's something important.

I own a dvd and I watch it often, it just hits a mood sometimes 
9. The "Hole in the hand" moment. Before CGI proper... 

## 2. just watched THE MEDIUM (2021)

**Score:** 32
**Body:** Just watched \*The Medium\* and it genuinely freaked me out.

I’ve seen tons of Hollywood horror and almost nothing scares me anymore, but this one was different. The slow, realistic build just made it worse and worse.

Ended up sleeping with the lights on… which never happens for me.

Anyone else felt the same? Also open to similar recommendations.



### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You should immediately watch 'The Wailing' then. Its a South Korean film by Na Hong-jin, and he is the same guy who co-wrote and produced 'The Medium'. 

I liked The Medium as well, but The Wailing is a true masterpiece.
2. Highly recommend you Incantation 
  - Great film! I love it! It is a very clever and tense watch!
3. Sorry this is a Starbucks, we call it a grande here.
4. Thete were theaters in Thailand that played the movie with the house lights on for people because it was so scary. 
5. I have two complete different experiences with this movie
My first watch I stopped cause I couldn't bare it after like 30 min I would say and had difficulty to chill the whole night

Second watch, didn't give a shit about anything and slept like a baby

I don't know what happend, still pretty solid movie
6. .
7. Yeah I just watched the trailer and I might nope out of this one 😅 It looks terrifying
8. Noroi and the whole Koji Shiraishi output on YouTube 
9. They've commissioned a sequel. The Large

## 3. Looking for movies with important social or political issues :)

**Score:** 23
**Body:** Hiiii! My English professor gave us an assignment to write a two page paper about a movie of our choosing that showcases social or political issues. I haven’t got a clue wha to watch 😭 if anyone has anything they recommend please share 👌

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. For economic class divides, consider the Korean film, Parasite 
2. [Gattaca](https://reelgood.com/movie/gattaca-1997) - This covers a *lot* of the subjects in your assignment... genetic engineering, totalitarian government, invasive technology, class divides, and individual vs. group welfare that I can recall of the top of my head.  And it's a great film, to boot.  A non-genetically engineered man wants to go to space, so he assumes the identity of a genetically engineered recluse to further his goals.
3. 12 Angry Men. The prejudices that we don’t realize we have.
4. V for Vendetta 

Equilibrium 

Dark City

Wag the Dog

Driving Miss Daisy 

Green book 

Schindler's List
5. Don’t look up
6. GATTACA covers at least four of those topics.
7. Promising Young Woman
8. There's so many ways to go...

The Island

Of Mice and Men

The Matrix

The Milagro Beanfield War

Erin Brockovich

Its A Wonderful Life 

Logan's Run

Running Man

Wag the Dog

Total Recall




9. The Grapes of Wrath
10. Norma Rae

## 4. Movies set entirely on boats?

**Score:** 24
**Body:** I like movies that are set in one space... like a single room or on a train.. but how about good movies set on boats? Save your keyboard there's no need to reply Titanic we all know that one ;)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dead Calm
2. All Is Lost


3. Poseidon Adventure. 
4. The African Queen?
5. Lifeboat (1944)
6. Ghost Ship.  Watch the first five minutes, then you can turn it off.

Edit to add that Triangle and Sea Fever are both fairly decent boat horror movies.
7. \- Triangle (2009)

\- The Perfect Storm (2000)

\- Dead Calm (1989)

\- Virus (1999)

\- Master and Commander (2003)
  - Master and Commander is so underrated 
8. Speed 2: cruise control
9. Monkey Business (1931) - Marx Brothers vehicle set on a transatlantic crossing.

Mr. Roberts (1955) - a Navy captain is disgruntled about his assignment to a supply ship in the Pacific during WW II.

Moby Dick (1956) - told as a series of flashbacks that are aboard the ship.

The Old Man and the Sea (1958) - Spencer Tracy miscast in Hemingway's tale of a poor, lone fisherman driven to land the biggest fish of his life.

Das Boot (1981) - set inside a German U-boat during WW II.

In the Heart of the Sea (2015) - a retelling of the actual events that inspired Moby-Dick



## 5. I'm looking for movies that are very attention grabbing and fast paced

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I've just watched several slow burn things and I'm sick of it, I want something that has almost no "dead air". Something with a lot of action, or dialogue, or lots of flashy visuals. also, Where the story constantly progresses, and/or keeps me in suspense.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. my dinner w andre. jk jk. Fury road is fast paced but you've prob seen.

check out - Snowpiercer, Upgrade, You're next, Train to Busan, Green Room

if you want horror - Evil dead rise & Where evil lurks
2. Scott Pilgrim Vs The World
3.  Raid and Raid 2  
  - Love the Raid
4. What?! 
Not the obvious yet?

Mission:Impossible!!

Those Movies are like a Rollercoaster Ride where it only goes up :D

  - It's Tom Cruise, so there's that against it.
5. Try Mad Max: Fury Road, Uncut Gems, or John Wick - all super fast-paced.
6. Anything with Jason Statham
7. Death Wish 3
8. Run Lola Run, Speed, Dredd, Crank 1&2

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Happy 49th birthday to Jessica Chastain! Do you have a favorite character she played?

**Score:** 517
**Body:** I’ll start. Adult Murphy Cooper in Interstellar and Commander Melissa Lewis in The Martian 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Zero Dark 30
2. Molly
3. She was fantastic in The Help
4. Murph. 
DON’T LET ME LEAVE MURPH! 
5. Lawless
6. ![gif](giphy|4tV8i44d3mK1G)

Zero Dark Thirty

My favorite thing about her is she's just some regular as person. Not a nepo, she grew up half poor like the rest of us. Beautiful, and fantastic actress.
7. Mollys Game. 
8. I first noticed Maggie Beaufort in Lawless, but Molly's Game is even best.
9. Interstellar
10. Zero dark 30 and Molly's game. 10/10

## 2. Who is the most Likeable Character in film history

**Score:** 402
**Body:** Not the SMARTEST not the most ATTRACTIVE not the most MYSTERIOUS 

Just the charcter you would want to be your neighbor or date your sibling. 



### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|PudZiAbQDUEik|downsized)
  - OP said not the most attractive.
2. ![gif](giphy|3ornjMd3Pu2F8Vi7DO)
3. https://preview.redd.it/0robk0tmmxqg1.jpeg?width=500&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=e780e0be84b61759d1b50013c7cde85f89fd3689
4. ![gif](giphy|3oGRFmZWP8yqUexvK8)

How can Atticus Finch not be at the top of this list?
5. Aragorn son of Arathorn 
6. ![gif](giphy|RNSWaC17LX91C|downsized)
7. ![gif](giphy|g67pETponv03e)

Samwise Gamgee
8. Julie andrews as ..well, I could pick a couple.   I will go with Mary Poppins .  Wonderful neighbor material. 
9. ![gif](giphy|bq6F8QYqBU7Yc)



## 3. What's Woody Harrelson best film?

**Score:** 233
**Body:** I would say The Messenger (2009), but what about yours?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/xxz2lttu9yqg1.png?width=576&format=png&auto=webp&s=310d51c4a156d22f15c3a0cf97c481dacdf295a6


2. White men can’t jump, what a hustler 
  - Him and Snipes were absolutely perfect together.
3. Can we please just focus on Rampart.
4. ![gif](giphy|EvpXUazqU5e3S)

Zombieland bruh
5. ![gif](giphy|l2Vkcg35q5z7G)

Natural Born Killers
6. Not a film but his performance in True detective was unreal. You could literally feel how unbearable what he was seeing was when McConaughey made him watch the tape.
7. ![gif](giphy|Zs4gZUu1cOpt6)
8. People vs Larry Flint
9. Three billboards was quite deep and well acted role. 

## 4. It’s kind of wild that Alfred Hitchcock who is one of the most influential film directors never won an Oscar

**Score:** 160

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hitchcock was a fantastic filmmaker and whilst it's an oddity that he never won an Oscar it doesn't detract from his legendary status in the slightest.
  - Sidney Lumet never won a competitive Oscar.
  - One could argue that it detracts from the Oscar and/or the way winners are chosen for the award
2. he didn’t care, his legacy was bigger than some golden statue.
  - I recently read a couple behind the scenes books about Hitchcock and he definitely did care a lot about never winning. He thought he was going to win for Psycho but Billy Wilder won instead for The Apartment which was the final nail in the coffin for him on the Oscar’s apparently
3. rebecca was his best chance I believe, but I feel like kubrick not having an oscar is also wild 
  - He won the best special effects for 2001. That's how you know Oscar is just a game.
4. Neither did Kurosawa. Both absolute legends 
5. Kubrick, Hitchcock, Lumet …. Says a lot about the academy.
6. His genre was never popular for Oscars and it was an achievement that he was nominated for best director a number of times. Joan Fontaine is the only actor/actress to receive an Oscar for a Hitchcock movie.

## 5. My top 20 favorite movies of all time:

**Score:** 125
**Body:** What do you think of this list?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is the Courtyard by Marriot of movie lists.
2. I'm not sure why people are being so rude to you.   It's a fine list, probably not exactly the one I would create.   
  - because it's basically IMDB's top 20
3. This almost comes off as someone being a little pretentious about their movie tastes rather than genuine.  A lot of the movies are films that are critically acclaimed, but not as popular with audiences, which might hint at someone just looking to appear cultured and sophisticated, or perhaps a first year film student that is trying too hard to look like they get it.

That being said, none of the films on here are inherently bad films, so I will give you the benefit of the doubt and assume it's genuine.  Just thought I'd share that it looks, at first glance, to be a overly pretentious list that not even a professional movie critic would likely come up with.
4. Nice list. Surprised godfather Part 2 didn't make top 20. 
5. First decent list ive seen here
6. You might want to try something in another language friend. There’s a whole world of cinema out there. 
  - This is very good advise. South Korea and Japan might be interesting to you OP. 
    - I can offer a short list of some of the best non-english films I've seen.

I think op top 20 list might look a little different after seeing these 🙂

City of God

The Seventh Seal

La Haine

Spirited Away 

Beau Travail 

Portrait of a Lady on Fire 

Parasite

8 1/2

In the mood for love

Petite Maman
      - Thanks for the list man...most of these are on my watchlist too except Seventh Seal and Petite Maman. Ive seen Parasite and City of God only. I guess you've seen the usual like Oldboy, Memories of Murder, Incendies, Cure, Cache, Climax, the Handmaiden etc? Maybe check out Speak No Evil, Elite Squad 1 and 2, Rec 1 and 2 too.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. *Advice* I think my screenwriter has secretly made his script with AI. What do I do?!

**Score:** 58
**Body:** Hi guys, I came here to ask a question from a concerned indie director about a Script that might possibly be written by AI or Co-Assisted. (this is my first debut film)

I've been working on a horror feature film for nearly a year now and has now finally gotten out of pre-production (the film is based on the screenwriters book that he has written "apparently" but has also used AI Art for the book). But as shooting has started and I've started to analyze the script more and more for our upcoming scenes to shoot I've found bits and pieces of code that according to my own research seem to be related to AI generated LLM responses. (To be specific bits of code such as "Markdown" with several instances being found within the script.) Upon confronting my screenwriter/producer if he had other drafts he told me "he doesn't have any drafts for his script, only the drafts for the book" which was based off the screenplay.

Am I wrong for thinking this is a massive red flag?

Is it normal to not have a single draft for a screenplay?

I'm considering confronting him, and if it is true I'll be stepping away from the project entirely as I don't want my work or my debut film to be affiliated with AI.

What do you all think I should do? I came here as a lot of you are very experienced and would know better judgement of this than my own morals and ethics.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It sounds sketchy as hell but dude, why are you making a film based on a script that you don’t seem to be … all that familiar with?

Like ok, it’s written by AI but you haven’t made any comments about the quality of the story, which makes me think that this film might not turn out to be all that great to begin with
  - Yeah idk why you’d pay someone to write a script when you don’t know how good their writing samples are? And what is someone doing in preproduction for a whole year that doesn’t involve actually looking at the script? Fining actors, locations, putting shot lists together, etc, all involves getting pretty familiar with the script.

AI isn’t really anywhere near what a good screenwriter can do. At a minimum it should have been obvious if the script just wasn’t good. 

Also, as a writer, I probably wouldn’t be willing to share old “working drafts” unless it was related to payment and whether something was revisions or a full rewrite. People don’t want sub-par examples of their work floating around for no reason.
2. I don't doubt your conclusions are correct, but one thing to be aware of is that markdown predates AI and has many uses outside of AI.There are certainly some non-linear outlining tools like logseq that are used by writers that use markdown. And lots of content management tools use markdown.

Doesn't mean your writer hasn't used AI of course, it's just not conclusive by itself.
  - Markdown is popular with screenwriters. Specifically the app Fountain, which was developed by John August who has a very influential and widely listened to screenwriting podcast. 
3. Ask him for details. Pick a scene or a seemingly insignificant small detail and ask “Why was this detail important?” “What motivated the character to do this?” or “What inspired you to think to put this here?” Just ask questions like that and see if he gives thoughtful answers. If it’s really his story, he’ll probaly be happy to talk about it more. If a bot slopped it up for him, he may not have answers at all because he never did the thinking to put the story together. He may not even know his own story.
  - Tbh I think this is more plausible, as he couldn't give me any answers regarding why he doesn't have multiple drafts, and why some things within the script are written. He said "It's been awhile since I've revisited the script and I can't really give you an answer unfortunately."

One of these times in particular an actress we met with for rehearsals was asking about a part in the later half of the script which mentions 7 candles being lit, with each candle representing those that have died throughout the story not including the protagonist who is speculated throughout the film to be the final candle etc. (the screenwriter not realising that only 5 people have died throughout the story which I thought was a simple mistake at the time)

our actress brought up a really good question which was "what did these candles represent?"

He responded "it's meant to represent the 6 people that have died throughout the story including the protagonist who is rumoured to die"

She rebutted with "but only 5 people die throughout the story including yourself? So it'd be 6 correct?"

He quickly replied with "oh I actually meant 6 people as it's supposed to resemble a pentagram"

Our actress (who took 16th century witchcraft for university):
"A pentagram is 5..."

*Silence*

Yeah to be honest, I'm angry with myself that I didn't put the dots together that day. He doesn't even know his own script.
    - You don’t even need to have studied witchcraft to know a PENTAgram is five points. Like a PENTAgon. Unless he was thinking of the Star of David, that would open a whole other can of worms.
    - Sounds like he used AI extensively or is a really crappy, unprofessional writer. Can you hire another writer for rewrites? That should clear the AI element and give you a better result 
4. I work in tv development and have read thousands of scripts and worked on 10+ seasons of produced television. I also write my own notes in markdown, so I’m familiar with the language. You can dm me the script and I’ll tell you if it’s AI. 

The most suspicious part is not having old drafts. They definitely do, they just don’t want to share it with you for some reason. 
  - Not just old drafts, but treatments, character notes, etc. Writing a screenplay is not something that a person can just sit down and do in one sitting (unless they're on a cocaine binge and if so, the screenplay will read like it was written on a cocaine binge). 

## 2. Why does it feel like older films are more deliberately composed?

**Score:** 54
**Body:** I’m an artist, but I was never really big on film. I recently started watching a bunch of the “classics,” like Rear Window, Stalker, Vertigo, Seven Samurai, etc. In some of the movies I listed, it feels like every single shot is thought out and intentionally composed. You could legitimately take a screenshot of certain scenes, and they’d stand on their own. I was wondering why you personally think this is and why it is much rarer to see in modern films.

EDIT: I feel like the same can be said for animation. I also recently watched Evangelion and mostly felt the same.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It has always been rare, you are just taking the handful of older films that have stood the test of time as classics and comparing them to the countless films you see now. You are not considering the countless generic boring thoughtless movies that came out in the same time periods as those classics that nobody today knows or cares about 
  - Survivorship bias is a big one. People always make the argument that cinema was better before, and maybe they’re right to some degree, but if you really take a step back and look, we still get a ton of modern classics recent years gone by. People will probably say the same thing about the 2010’s and 2020’s fifty years from now
    - r/CineShots set me straight. Cinematography and composition are not lost arts, it's just harder to know where to look without the benefit of hindsight. 
      - Precisely why I [started this collection](https://boxd.it/vZgzI). Most top x cinematography of all time lists are usually pre 2000
    - Maybe but there’s something to be said for the lifecycle of an art form , in and of itself and also how it intersects with commerce ; just the changes over time to how things are done I don’t think lends itself to clear cut analogs
  - >It has always been rare

But not *always to the same degree*. Big, popular blockbusters used to be shot like that and now it hardly ever happens; I'm sure you can find a similar trend in smaller or independent films as well.
2. you've just mentioned four of the best movies ever made. the classics that you're watching are the best of their time. it was also exceptionally rare back then, you're just not watching the bad ones. 

there are some other factors: 

\- shooting digitally allows you to, if you want, to be less careful with the light and less careful with the frame because it's much cheaper to keep shooting and shooting. 

\- acting styles have changed. starting in the 70s, as performers and filmmakers embraced naturalism, rigidly blocking every moment for a perfect shot isn't always worth what you lose in the feel of the performance. 

  
but mostly, you're just only watching the best movies from back them and assuming that they were all like that. there are plenty of movies today that meet the 'every frame a painting' standard. 
  - I think your second point is the “best answer”, because there’s plenty of great filmmakers today whose films aren’t classically beautiful, they’re trying to be realistic and natural. Like, of course a Dardenne film isn’t going to be deliberately composed in the way a Hitchcock film is. Why would you want it to be? Terrence Malick has made a lot of gorgeous looking films with painterly still frames that very literally are the result of improvisation, nothing deliberate about it. 
3. I watch A LOT of old obscure movies. I can assure you it has nothing to do with survivorship bias. Random made for TV movies have more thought put into them then many big blockbusters today.

Don't let people try to say its only the well known movies. If anything the lesser known movies are often better than the classics! And obviously I'm speaking on average, there are bad classic movies too and good modern ones.
4. I think we rely too much on post these days. Blocking and composition… the idea that every frame can be a painting is no longer an art form in blockbuster Hollywood films. 

These things still exist in major ways across many modern films. Hamnet is the first to come to mind that reminds me of the intricately thought out production. Others that come to mind are 

Dreams (1990),
Train Dreams,
Parasite,
The Secret Agent,
In The Mood for Love,
Die My Love,
Sentimental Value,
Bugonia,
Sinners,
Eden (2024),
Blade Runner 2049,
Nosferatu (2024),
Mother!,
There Will Be Blood,
The Revenant,
Frida (2002)

Honestly I could go on and on. There are lots of stunning modern films with excellent composition, blocking, and lighting. It’s just not common in blockbusters

## 3. Perfect Days by Wim Wenders is my favorite film. I just found out some people don't find it sad?

**Score:** 32
**Body:** I am kind of baffled that people find this not to be a sad film as in my opinion it is the saddest I have ever seen.

  
Recently after watching it again for the nth time I was googling it afterwards as you do and I found a frightening amount of people wanting to be like the main character and/or finding the movie to be peaceful and happy.

  
Am I in the minority with my opinion on it being as sad as it is? To me it is much more sad than something like Grave of the Fireflies but that might be controversial.

  
Does anyone have any insight on this? I am curious about the other perspective.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. You may feel sad because of the movie, but the movie itself is not simply sad. Hirayama explores many different emotions, an I think embodies a simple manner of living that he takes pride in and finds meaningful. The sadness comes in his neices strained relationship with her mother, and with the disappointment Hirayama finds when people try to take advantage. But he’s very fulfilled, I think the film is trying to show us that a simple life can be meaningful and the small moments when we devote our full attention to what’s in front of us give it that meaning, rather than grand gestures and anticipations. 
  - Interesting. Maybe it's something inside of me personally that can't imagine him as being fulfilled or happy when watching the film.
2. The best thing about this movie is that it doesn't tell you what to think. Your own state of mind will color the film, I find it full of melancholy, but there is a certain contentment that borders on a joyful enlightenment. Satisfaction with a simple life that is missing from our modern lives.
3. I don't find it "happy" or "sad." In my opinion, the movie highlights the complexity of emotions, and the ways they can coexist. Sometimes there is sadness in our contentment, just like there is love in our grief. 
4. He finds peace in his job. There's so many movies that go deep into loss, grief, war, etc. and maybe this movie touches on a few of those things, but he lives a life that he largely likes and nothing crazy happens to him. I wasn't struck at it being particularly sad or happy, but it was very compelling.
5. How dare others find different emotions through filmmaking?!

I’ve seen the movie a few times and liked it but the ending feels like contentment to me. A man later in life who’s trying to make amends with certain areas in his life. There may be sadness there but that wasn’t the overall expression i took from it. 
6. I think it is bittersweet, like much of life is; that’s exactly what I love about it. For me, it’s about how you find a way to live life, to create meaning for your life in a world that will not give it to you. It is, in the end, a very existentialist film. 
7. It's one of my favourite modern movies and I found it to be one of the most uplifting and life-affirming films I've seen in such a long time, it makes you certain you can find happiness and peace in the small, simple things in life.
8. As I see it, the character is fictional yet written and acted in such a way that he feels real. This in and of itself pleases me. Also, the cinematography was superb, kind of a whole other level - another aspect which was pleasing to me. On top of that all this was taking place in a city I’ve never been too, so that aspect too was a “big plus” for me. And then let’s not forget all the incidents that transpired within its runtime, which were all depicted with a very humanistic, compassionate and poetic touch. All of all that, taken together, adds up to me as a viewer being uplifted and being made grateful that there are artists such as Wim Wenders (among many others) out there that “get it.” 

No sadness, just real, actual lifestuff being portrayed honestly.
9. For me it was extremely emotional at times but not sad. And you mention some described the movie as peaceful, and it is because the main character made peace with himself and the world. 

My favorite, and the most impactful scene to me was the one where he meets with his sister, and he embraces her in spite of their strained relationship and her being a bit judgmental of his life….I found that to be actually quite profoundly uplifting. It was like he was telling her that he was satisfied with his life even though she might feel like there was a reason for him not to be. And with that out of the way, they could simply embrace as brother and sister.


## 4. The Lives of Others: The Silent Rebellion of Conscience

**Score:** 29
**Body:** "Sometimes a person’s life changes in an instant. They begin to question the values they have believed in for years after an unexpected event. **“The Lives of Others**” is precisely the story of such transformation-the silent rebellion of conscience.

The film **“The Lives of Others”**, or by its original title **“Das Leben der Anderen”**, which won the **“Oscar”** for Best Foreign Language Film in 2007, covers the years 1984–1991 in the German Democratic Republic. It portrays the moral and political situation in East and West Germany.

In fact, the film is a depiction of a society experiencing social and moral trauma, reflected through individuals. During that period, like all other fields, the theater, which was completely censored, had also been turned into a tool of state propaganda. It was impossible to continue one’s art without cooperating with the state. People were afraid of being removed from their careers, imprisoned, or perhaps worst of all, being isolated from society. These pressures were not only a threat to one’s career. At the same time, they functioned as a mechanism that alienated a person even from themselves..."

[Read More](https://natigmamishov.medium.com/the-lives-of-others-the-silent-rebellion-of-conscience-9cb3707c1637)

If you have also watched it, it would be great to read what you thought.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What gets me about that movie is that it never turns conscience into a big speech. The shift is tiny, bureaucratic, almost humiliatingly small, and that is exactly why it feels real. A lesser film would make moral awakening look heroic. This one makes it look quiet, compromised, and late. The ending lands because the whole movie has trained you to understand how expensive even one decent act is in that system.
  - Compromised and late.. exactly! And I completely agree that the change in him being quiet and subtle has bigger effect and is more realistic.
2. As someone who grew up under Ceausescu, this felt like it managed to capture, with German specifics, the universally valid aspects of all such regimes. And it feels relevant even today, unfortunately.

Yes, as other comment says, for me, too the biggest positive surprise was that it does not have a big "aha! now I change" moment, there is no huge awakening, no plastic revelation, no didacticism. It does not teach either, it just lets the viewer (both those who had experience of such regimes and those who never had any awareness of these) gradually realise the biggest and most complex issue:

There are no solid lines between good and evil in such regimes... lines are very blurred, and it takes nations decades to process them after.

There are no simple definitions of evil either. It takes many forms across a whole scale... some do it by conviction, some forced by circumstances, some end up in it without realising. From outside, or later from a safe time period, others can pass simplistic judgements and make categorisations but... this has been much more complex.

The film for me is phenomenal because without a single explicit "teaching moment" it manages to convey and teach about all these aspects that people have been filling library shelves with ever since these regimes exited in the Eastern Bloc at least.

Plus... of course, the acting... sadly he passed away... his very restrained/controlled and interiorised manner of acting the part is absolutely awe-inspiring and an acting masterclass about expressing extremely intense and difficult inner states in such subdued manner.

And then the script is perfect... culminating with the closing lines in the book shop: "It is for me"... it still breaks me every time after dozens of viewings.
3. To me the movie is all about the random nature of human connection. About how feeling close to someone, being close with someone physically or conventionally, and actually having influence over their life are three completely different things that interact with each other in unpredictable and terrifying ways. I’ll admit I was expecting it to be all oscar-baity but it ended up blowing me away, watched it a year ago and still think about it often

## 5. Last Year at Marienbad - The Horror of Life

**Score:** 29
**Body:** I watched the movie because I saw it recommended somewhere and it got my attention, but I had no clear idea about what it was like, what it was about, or of any theories about the meanings of the story. I am writing this right after I saw it, and before I read any other opinions or analyses. I just want to talk about my own impression without implying it's necessarily right or intentional, which I don’t think is even important for this movie. Ambiguous movies often frustrate me and seem lazy, in this case it’s the opposite, I think the movie holds several stories and meanings that are being expressed through the same lines and images in a way I never saw before, holding them all in, like a true work of the subconscious. 

The way the movie is told, through constant repetitive narration over a melody, narration that still develops and changes enough to tell a story, quickly becomes hypnotic. The scenes and images over the narration, especially with that music, really feel like memories, key shots and frames you keep editing and slightly changing in your mind and returning to over and over again. 

I wrote about a movie here before, “Wings of Desire” that consists almost purely of random people’s thoughts, and instead of being boring just completely merges with your own thought patterns in some beautiful hypnotic way. This movie does it with a specific thought process, the obsessive narration of a memory that combines reality, fears, hidden moments you don’t want to recall, wishes for the future, for a different past, for the past you miss, all of it constantly repeating in your head. You’re stuck in a loop that’s always the same but always a little different, trying to find the way out. 

The hotel the story takes place in is the perfect background for these thoughts, with its ornaments, uniformity, it’s old, dark, heavy, beautiful too (I could start quoting the movie to describe it but I’ll restrain myself). It reminds me a little of The Shining, even with some plot elements, the interesting thing is that there you have the hedge maze where it's easy to get lost, and here the garden is very uniform and geometric, and it says it at first seems impossible to get lost in it. I know this sounds pretentious as fuck, but to me it sounds like getting lost in the monotony of life, where it's exactly the uniformity and repetition that confuse. 

While watching, and within the whole repetition of some moments and elements, I kept seeing several different stories. 

There is the general story. A man saw a woman a year ago at Marienbad. They had an affair and he wanted her to go away with him but she was married and told him to wait another year. Then he sees her again a year later and she pretends she doesn’t know him. He is reminding her of the past and how they met, and what happened then, but he is also narrating to her what is happening and what will happen now. She hesitates to leave, and eventually her husband kills her. 

Or, her husband killed her last year, and the lover stayed, trying to forget and waiting to see her again the next year. The woman he is talking to is her, and remembering him means remembering she’s dead. It is a horror of two lovers who are forever stuck in a loop inside of a hotel, where her hesitation got her killed, kept there by her refusal to remember that she died. This is why she shifts from not remembering him to remembering him, from engaging with him to begging him to leave her alone.  

Or, maybe the woman he is talking to is someone else completely and he is just projecting the past into her, wishing she was the woman from last year, and trying to change the past to a version where that woman didn’t die. Her reactions are mixed with the reactions of the woman in the past, and his fantasy that it is her. 

Sometimes it seems they are just two people who are playing a game of possibilities of what could have happened and still could happen between them. The murder didn’t really happen, it is just a possibility (or a metaphor) of what will be if she doesn’t leave the hotel with him.  

At one point of the movie I suddenly thought it was really her perspective, and the narrator is Death and her husband is Life. There is a moment after she had a panic attack and screamed, where she’s lying in the room and acting as if she doesn’t want to leave with her lover, she is asking for her husband for comfort. But the husband is shown as cold and hard to read, whose main trait is winning in a weird nonsensical game for which people keep offering contradictory strategies to win, and which he always wins. This part didn’t read as any kind of human relationship story at all, but as someone who wants the comfort of death, but is scared and keeps trying to pointlessly hold on to life which doesn’t offer any consolation. 

When “the lover” is asking her why she needs more time with her husband, why she needs another year, then later it’s hours, what she hopes will happen, and to just leave with him, it became a story of building the resolve to commit suicide. Life kills you. Death is the escape. But she is still trying to have a little more time with life. 

In the end they leave together, whether she killed herself or got finally taken by the death, or remembered that she was already dead, or just decided to stop the neurosis of possibilities by literally leaving with her lover, it all works and makes sense without any contradictions or confusions. Maybe it’s also many other things like that statue the characters discussed in the past, according to the narrator.

I consider this movie an excellent horror, and one of the more special movies I’ve ever seen. I'm glad I did, it feels personal in some way.
